title: Field of View Guide
summary: Understand how target distance, horizontal view angle, scene width, and mounting height affect what a camera can actually see.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates the practical horizontal coverage width at a given target distance and compares that view against the scene width you need to cover.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when deciding whether a proposed camera angle can cover a target area before you move into pixel density, lens selection, or camera spacing.
bullets: Early design planning.
Camera placement review.
Expansion or retrofit validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical physical security planning terms.

items.label: Target Distance (ft)
items.description: The horizontal planning distance from the camera to the target plane or area being evaluated.
items.whyItMatters: Distance is the base geometry for coverage width. The same angle covers more width as distance increases, but detail drops as the scene gets wider.
items.commonMistake: Using the farthest visible point instead of the actual target plane that must be identifiable.

links.label: Check detail after view width
links.note: Use Pixel Density after FOV to verify that the wider view still provides enough detail.

items.label: Horizontal FOV (deg)
items.description: The horizontal viewing angle of the camera/lens combination.
items.whyItMatters: A wider angle covers more scene but spreads pixels over more width. A narrower angle captures less scene but improves detail at the target.
items.commonMistake: Choosing the widest possible view and assuming it also provides recognition-quality detail.

links.label: Compare lens choice to view angle
links.note: Lens choice determines whether the desired view width is realistic for the target distance.

items.label: Scene Width to Cover (ft)
items.description: The width of the area you want the camera to cover at the target plane.
items.whyItMatters: Comparing required scene width to calculated view width shows whether one camera can cover the area or whether the scene needs a different lens/position.
items.commonMistake: Measuring the whole room instead of the specific target zone that needs usable coverage.

items.label: Mount Height (ft)
items.description: The installed camera height above the floor or grade.
items.whyItMatters: Mount height affects viewing angle, occlusion, facial view, and practical target geometry even when the horizontal FOV looks acceptable.
items.commonMistake: Mounting too high for tamper resistance and then losing useful detail on faces or plates.

links.label: Review height tradeoffs
links.note: Mounting Height helps evaluate whether the install height supports the intended target view.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the entered field of view appears to cover the scene with practical margin. Watch means the view may fit but should be checked for detail, height, or overlap. Risk means the proposed view is likely too narrow or too wide for the planning goal.
bullets: Coverage width tells you whether the scene physically fits in view.
Scene margin tells you whether the view has practical tolerance for aiming and field conditions.
A good FOV result should still be followed by pixel density and mounting review when detail matters.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most physical security planning problems come from treating camera coverage, recognition detail, mounting position, and lighting as separate decisions. In the field, those choices interact: a wider view lowers pixel density, a higher mount changes target geometry, and poor lighting can erase detail that looked acceptable on paper.
bullets: Sizing a camera view only by how wide the scene looks, without checking target detail.
Using clean daytime assumptions for a scene that must also work at night, in glare, or under motion.
Ignoring overlap, obstructions, mounting limits, and the actual target plane.
Treating calculated values as final design approval instead of documented planning assumptions.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: These guides explain the planning logic behind the tool. They do not replace manufacturer documentation, site survey, camera testing, client acceptance criteria, code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Validate against the actual camera model, lens, sensor, encoder settings, and mounting location.
Confirm lighting, reflections, obstructions, weather, and target movement before relying on a design.
Use calculated outputs as design evidence and review notes, not as a substitute for field validation.
Leave practical margin when the scene is important, difficult, or expected to change.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, on-site testing, legal review, privacy review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Document assumptions before implementation.
Review the design with the owner, integrator, manufacturer, and applicable authority when required.
Test representative views before treating a camera placement as final.
